data.title: AST SpaceMobile: The Pullback I Was Waiting For
data.description:
The problem here is not that one particular satellite failed. Launch delays are expected in any space venture; however, the market sometimes tends to treat them as anomalies. What makes AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) notable is the transition from the speculative business model to a business model that requires execution.

This is my explanation of the subsequent decline in the price after the BlueBird 7 episode. The problem here is not about the failure of the satellite, which performed normally when launched into orbit. This malfunction relates to the launch vehicle's poor performance.

However, this is a different case since the main issue now is not about the technology of the company. The key aspect here is that at this moment the company is becoming more dependent on external parties, especially on launches.

I am upgrading my earlier rating from Hold to Buy on the basis that the sell-off was an opportunity for resetting expectations rather than the thesis. The stock is now valued based on execution risk rather than perfect execution, as previously. The combination of technology, demand confirmation, and a solid balance sheet makes for an excellent risk-reward proposition.

Execution Reset, Not a Collapse

To make this clear, I was quite skeptical about investing in AST SpaceMobile prior to the news of the launch failure. The reason for this view was simple: the company's setup was simply getting ahead of itself. To that end, while I would not say that this is a bargain price range, the current decline represents much better buying grounds for me.

If I had to define the current pullback, I would rather call it a repricing of execution-related risks. ASTS had begun underperforming well before the launch date, which told me that there was an expectation for an event with binary results. Naturally, the launch malfunctioning confirmed the negative outcome.

The problem with the selloff is that the loss of BlueBird 7 means a lot more than just losing one more satellite. Whereas the company could come up with several versions of their product already, this specific piece was a component of Block 2 satellites that were much more powerful, at least one order of magnitude, than the previous versions. Loss of even one such piece may lead to some severe delays.

The malfunction of BlueBird 7 was caused by a failure in the launch rocket and had nothing to do with any deficiencies in the technological solutions provided by AST. It was the poor performance of the second stage that doomed this satellite to the orbit from which it would never be able to escape. This means that the satellite is now de-orbited.

While I don’t think it shatters the thesis statement of the project, I think that this problem should not be taken lightly either. The potential value creation by AST lies in its ability to rapidly develop a functional constellation, so this is very important here. One missing satellite will not break things, but one missed launch window might.

Ultimately, this failure highlights the critical nature of AST’s launch windows, meaning that the company will have far less margin for error moving forward and greater risk associated with the future launches. In other words, while the thesis is still intact, it definitely became a bit fragile for that matter.

Is the Target 2026 Deployment Still Achievable?

My assumption about the feasibility of the 2026 deployment is that it is possible, but with more difficulty than before. In the case of the failed BB7 satellite launch, the program had to be executed almost flawlessly. Today, however, it requires putting up around 45 satellites into orbit by 2026 due to fast production enabling multiple satellites to be manufactured per month.

The thing that keeps me up at night at this point is the margin. This approach is extremely dependent upon timing and synchronization. It’ll work, but there is no room for any sort of error. Each subsequent issue will add up and may result in failing to reach the deadline. The bottom line is that the goal is still feasible but with different circumstances. Before, it looked achievable with good implementation, while now it requires perfect execution.

Valuation Reset, Not Thesis Break

What makes ASTS interesting, in my view, is not that it has suddenly become cheap by traditional standards; it clearly hasn’t. The stock has a forward EV/Sales multiple of about 140, making it seem very pricey from any traditional valuation metric. However, I don’t believe this to be the complete story.

Ultimately, what the valuation stands for, at its core, is the confidence in the success of the transition from the stage of infrastructure construction (pre-revenue stage) to developing a scalable and profitable platform. Consensus forecasts suggest the company will be generating about ~$180 million in 2026, reaching the level of ~$1.8 billion in 2028 with more than $3 billion expected longer-term. As a result, the valuation multiples contract quite sharply towards the single-digit Price/Sales mark rather fast, which becomes much more reasonable.

Where I am betting at this moment is that the market might be overly discounting the execution risk after such a price decline. The company enjoys a solid balance sheet, no dilution risk in sight for now, and demand already proven through partnerships/contract backlog. This time around, however, I am not buying the stock just because it is statistically cheap. I am buying it because of the reset in risk/reward.

Launch Risk Will Decide the Upside

As I have already mentioned, it is very possible that AST SpaceMobile manages to get its satellites launched again. If this happens without further problems or disruptions, we should see the company's stock recovering rapidly. However, should it continue to experience troubles, I would need to revisit my thesis.

First of all, this would require further disruptions in the network deployment process. Second, the company should be unable to provide a concrete plan for future activities that will help the business to achieve its 2026 goal without any further problems. Otherwise, it will mean that AST is likely to suffer losses of some magnitude.

Bottom Line

I am now turning bullish on ASTS since I view this drop we have seen as an expectation reset and not a change in the fundamentals. The technology is working, there is proof that the demand exists, and there is no issue with the company’s balance sheet. What has changed is the attitude and the stock price.

data.title: Black Stone Minerals: May See Modest Benefits From Improved Long-Term Oil Prices
data.description:
Black Stone Minerals (BSM) has seen its unit price go down by over 10% since I last looked at it (in late February 2026). It is heavily hedged on both oil and natural gas in 2026, so near-term commodity price changes have little direct effect on its 2026 distributable cash flow. For example, a $40 increase in the average price of oil during 2026 would only increase Black Stone's 2026 distributable cash flow by three cents per unit, independent of any changes to its production levels.

However, the Middle East conflict has resulted in an increase in my long-term (after 2026) oil price expectations. That has increased from $70 to $75 (for WTI oil), translating into a small increase in my estimate of Black Stone's value.

I now estimate Black Stone's value at $16.00 per unit (up from $15.50 per unit) and consider it modestly undervalued now. This is based on long-term (after 2026) $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

Updated 2026 Outlook

The strip for 2026 is now around $80 WTI oil and $3.60 NYMEX natural gas. Black Stone is very heavily hedged though (roughly 88% on natural gas and 85% on oil), so there is only a minor direct impact on its free cash flow from changes in commodity prices.

At the current strip, Black Stone is projected to generate $420 million in revenues, inclusive of negative $32 million in 2026 hedge value.

At an average of $100 WTI oil in 2026 instead, Black Stone's projected distributable cash flow would increase to $276 million (around $1.30 per unit) if there was no change to its production levels. Thus a $20 per barrel increase in oil prices would only directly increase its 2026 distributable cash flow by $3 million.

Black Stone's distribution coverage is thus likely to be around 1.05x to 1.1x for 2026 (with a $0.30 per unit quarterly distribution) if production ends up near its guidance midpoint. Production variance will have a much larger effect on its 2026 distributable cash flow than commodity prices.

Future Hedges

As of late February 2026, Black Stone's hedge position was smaller in 2027 compared to 2026. It has approximately 46% of its 2027 oil production hedged and 44% of its 2027 natural gas production hedged if its 2027 production is flat compared to its 2026 guidance midpoint.

Black Stone does expect some future natural gas production growth though, so its current 2027 natural gas hedges may cover around 40% of its natural gas production that year.

Black Stone's 2027 natural gas hedges are at a solid average price of $3.93, while its 2027 oil hedges are at a relatively low average price of $59.64.

At my long-term commodity prices of $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas, Black Stone's 2027 hedges would have approximately $16 million in negative value.

Notes On Valuation

My prior estimate of Black Stone's value was $15.50 per unit based on long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

The improvement in 2026 oil prices has a negligible effect on Black Stone's estimated value due to its substantial hedging. Black Stone's 2026 distributable cash flow can only realistically vary by a few cents per unit through commodity price changes alone.

I have increased my long-term price for oil by $5, though. Although Black Stone's oil cut is expected to be only 23% in 2026 and lower in future years (with production growth being driven by natural gas), that is still enough to increase its estimated value to $16 per unit.

This is based on long-term (after 2026) $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas, and also factors in the negative impact of Black Stone's current hedges.

Conclusion

Black Stone Minerals isn't seeing much benefit from strong near-term oil prices due to its hedges. It is roughly 85% hedged on oil in 2026, and production costs and ad valorem taxes are expected to be close to 10% of its oil and gas revenues before hedges. As a result, approximately only 5% of any 2026 oil price increase would filter through to Black Stone's distributable cash flow.

I do believe that the Iran conflict will have a longer-term impact on oil prices, though, and have thus increased my long-term (after 2026) WTI oil price from $70 to $75.

This helps slightly improve my estimate of Black Stone's value to $16 per unit (from $15.50 per unit).

data.title: Polestar: Delivery Growth With A Steep Price
data.description:
Polestar Automotive Holding UK PLC (PSNY) has seen its fair share of troubles in recent years, with the EV enterprise seeing major demand headwinds, including the elimination of U.S. federal tax incentives for the purchase of an electric vehicle in September 2025 and weak delivery projections. There was also a 1-for-30 reverse stock split, effective December 9, 2025, that was completed to avoid a delisting from the Nasdaq.

Polestar reported full-year results last week that showed that the business is gaining some traction, although profitability challenges remain. The EV enterprise reported record deliveries for 2025 but is still losing a lot of money on each EV sold to customers. Shares of the EV enterprise dropped 12% following the earnings report, however, indicating that investors still worry about the firm's high cash burn and net losses. While I like the improvement in certain financial figures for Polestar (revenues and deliveries specifically), I believe the risk profile is not favorable.

Previous rating

I rated shares of Polestar a Sell in my last work on the EV enterprise—1-For-30 Stock Split Changes Nothing—because of the firm’s high reliance on external funding as well as unfavorable profitability setup. Polestar did see a higher delivery volume in FY 2025, but its gross losses also expanded, and its gross profit margins are still very much negative, meaning the enterprise is losing money on every vehicle it sells. Since the U.S. recently also eliminated the $7,500 tax credit for the purchase of an EV, I believe the investment setup right now is not appealing for investors.

Strong delivery ramp, but profit situation remains challenged

Polestar sold 60,119 cars in the retail sector in 2025, showing a 34.0% year-over-year growth rate. In FY 2024, the EV platform—which is primarily focused on the manufacturing of high-performing, up-market sport utility vehicles that are fully battery-powered—sold 44,851 EVs to customers, mainly in Europe and the U.S. Polestar’s full-year results were mainly driven by the success of the Polestar 3 and Polestar 4 EV models, which were available to customers throughout the entire fiscal year.

As a result, the EV company reported $3.1B in revenues for FY 2025, a record for Polestar, showing 50% year-over-year growth. While this growth and the denser SUV-centric product portfolio are clear wins for the company, Polestar is still losing money on each vehicle sold in the retail market. In fact, the company reported a gross loss of $1.1B for FY 2025, which calculates to a loss of ~$18,000 per vehicle sold.

While losing money on each vehicle is not out of the ordinary for an EV start-up, the persistence of losses and increase in gross losses vs. FY 2024 (by 24%) is a major headwind for the company and could prevent a major revaluation to the upside for the EV company.

For the current year, Polestar said that it expects ”low double-digit” retail volume growth in FY 2026, which implies a delivery figure of 66,000 to 72,000 units, or 69,000 EVs at the midpoint. Since the consensus revenue expectation for the current fiscal year—according to Seeking Alpha, provided estimates—sits at ~$3.84B, the market currently implies that Polestar will lose about $19,500 per vehicle in 2026, assuming that the EV company maintains negative gross margins of ~35%. For comparison, Polestar sold 44,581 EVs globally, which means the company also lost about $19,500 per EV on a gross profit basis.

This lack of progress in the profitability structure is a major concern for me. Related to this, Polestar has a high cash burn in its core EV manufacturing business—it spent a total of $1.44B in FY 2025—meaning it relies on external funding to finance its production ramp.

In the most recent quarter, Polestar had a cash balance of $1.16B, showing 57% year-over-year growth, mainly due to equity injections of $500M in the second half of the year, as well as $1.26B in new loan facilities and other financing adjustments that took place throughout the year. At the current cash burn rate of ~$360M per quarter—to finance R&D, manufacturing costs, and its global expansion—Polestar has a short liquidity runway of ~3 quarters, and the EV company will likely have to raise more capital in 2026, potentially diluting shareholders further.

Polestar’s valuation

The EV enterprise is currently trading at a forward price-to-revenue ratio of 6.6X, which is extremely high and, in my opinion, not justifiable given the weak liquidity and gross profit situation at Polestar. For comparison, Rivian Automotive, Inc. (RIVN) and Lucid Group, Inc. (LCID) are trading at P/S ratios of 1.9X and 0.5X, respectively. Rivian recently achieved positive gross profits per vehicle—Return To Growth In 2026—while Lucid has one of the worst gross margins in the industry, and the company's valuation reflects this.

Given Polestar's weak profitability situation, I do not believe an above-average 6.6X revenue multiplier can be justified. Valuing PSNY more appropriately at the industry group average P/S ratio of ~3.0X, which itself may be high given the lack of profitability in the EV start-up industry, would yield a fair value closer to $8.20 per share vs. a current share price of $18.06.

Risks with PSNY

The biggest risk with Polestar relates to its elevated cash burn and high losses in its core manufacturing business. While the company managed to raise equity capital last year (discussed in my last work on the EV company), Polestar is likely going to have to raise more cash to finance its production ramp of the Polestar 3 and Polestar 4 models. This obviously poses dilution risk for investors and could lead to more pressure on the company's valuation factor if the company announces new equity raises in 2026. What may change my opinion on Polestar—potentially leading to a hold or buy rating—is if the EV platform were to drastically lower its (gross) losses per vehicle sold and improve its gross profit margins.

Final thoughts

Polestar saw some much-needed positive delivery momentum and an associated revenue surge of 50% Y/Y in its 2025 fiscal year. However, the gross margin situation remains strained, and the company is losing a ton of money on each vehicle sold. The implication for 2026, assuming that the EV company were to maintain stable gross profit margins, is that Polestar could lose up to $20,000 for each vehicle sold. The lack of a significant improvement in the profitability setup, as well as associated dilution risks, are two reasons why I am sticking to my sell rating for PSNY, despite a significant upswing in deliveries last year. Given the backdrop of its reverse stock split and continual high cash burn, I believe the PSNY risk profile remains unattractive for investors.

data.title: Tesla Q1 Earnings Preview: Ready For Its Next Big Move
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) currently trades at a forward P/E ratio of ~197x (Seeking Alpha data). If all it took to select winning stocks was reading a P/E ratio, I could end my coverage with this sentence, assigning a "STRONG SELL" rating.

Yet, I think Alpha is not generated in reading financial ratios. Rather, it is generated by asking oneself what the market sees in a stock, trying to predict sentiment and momentum before these manifest.

Ahead of Tesla's Q1 earnings, I make the case we may be 1 to 4 quarters away from a catalyst that will radically change market sentiment for this company, igniting a new bull run.

Tesla is the only Tech manufacturer in the West

There is no doubt in my mind the market doesn't see Tesla as an automaker, but rather as a Tech company. And not any Tech company, but the only Tech manufacturer in the West.

I find there is simply no other American or European company that has the same manufacturing capabilities of Tesla. Most "Mag7" companies are software houses, and even those who are into hardware like Apple, Inc. (AAPL) or NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) do not directly operate their factories, relying on third parties, often located outside of the US or Europe.

The few companies that do own and operate manufacturing capabilities within the US or Europe, like ASML Holding N.V. (ASML) or Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN), specialize in highly sophisticated products (microchips and machines to produce said microchips).

To find a direct competitor to Tesla, one needs to look at Asia. In China, companies like Xiaomi Corporation (XIACF) have similar manufacturing and software capabilities to Tesla. In South Korea, Hyundai and Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (SSNLF) can also partially compete.

The Tesla ecosystem is now positioned to automate the world

Since my last coverage, Musk's ecosystem has evolved significantly. Tesla's CEO had his other company, SpaceX, acquire xAI and bring X (the social media platform) under the same umbrella. He also announced Terafab, a joint project between Tesla, SpaceX and Intel Corporation (INTC), which joined in April.

The infographic below, which I updated since my last work, outlines the footprint of Musk's companies and how central Tesla's role is in that vision.

I see this consolidation as great news for Tesla's shareholders. My long term, hyper-bullish target price for Tesla is a $10 Trillion valuation (I will cover it later in this article). Ultimately, I believe such a valuation can be achieved only if the company plays a central role in the upcoming AI automation wave, vertically integrating its whole automation "supply chain" (from chips to software to data collection and data consolidation, to robotics). Joint efforts with SpaceX are a welcome development to accelerate such a colossal integration.

Tesla's moat is unbeatable

At the time of writing, Musk's ecosystem (included but not limited to Tesla) is able to independently design and manufacture:

  • EV cars with Full Self-Driving software with the full stack, from raw silicon (AI5 chip) to the vehicle itself.

  • Battery Megapacks.

  • Rockets and spaceships via SpaceX.

  • AI chips via the newly announced Terafab project.

  • Humanoid robots (Optimus Gen 3 enters production in summer 2026 at GigaShanghai)

  • Satellite connectivity with Starlink.

  • Social and AI infrastructure (including LLMs) with X and xAI.

As I just outlined, there are very few companies that can potentially match such capabilities. What makes this moat unbeatable is not any single capability. Rather, it is the combinatorial effect. Most Tech players could never shift their manufacturing ability to start producing, for example, robots. Tesla can, because its Gigafactories are software-defined, its supply chain is internalized, and its AI stack is fully proprietary.

The era of "physical AI" is still far, but Tesla is playing that game

Since I started covering AI and related stocks on Seeking Alpha, I have repeated the same "mantra" about how the AI race is evolving: from simple chatbots to generative AI, to agentic AI and finally to physical AI. The picture above, which I used extensively in my past coverage, outlines exactly that vision.

Tesla is, in my opinion, the best play on the last step of this race: physical AI. We are, by all accounts, still relatively far from that era. Q1 earnings will not necessarily bring important updates to the long term vision. However, investing in Tesla means doing an early bet on the West's only real player in the physical AI field. For me personally, this is why I consider exposure to Tesla as a non-negotiable in my portfolio.

How Tesla could grow into a $10 Trillion valuation

In one of my past articles on Tesla, I performed a "reverse engineer" exercise, discussing what the company would look like if it were to grow well beyond its current market valuation. My long term stretch target for that exercise was (and remains today) that of a $10 Trillion company.

This target (which I am not providing with a specific timeframe) is based on what size Tesla's business segment would need to grow into to justify a $10 trillion valuation with the below assumptions:

  • A terminal P/E ratio of 35X for Tesla, which is in line with Apple, Inc.'s (AAPL) historical premium valuation, even after the company's growth slowed into single digits.

  • A net profit margin (net income) of ~20% company wise, slightly below that of Apple today.

The results of what a $10 Trillion Tesla might look like by segment size (revenue and Net Income) are summarized in the table below.

This model would see a company generating a whopping $285 Billion in Net Income and roughly double the total revenue of Walmart Inc. (WMT) today. The sole revenue from Robotics (or any other single segment) would be more than double the total revenue of Tesla today.

Admittedly, this is a stretched scenario. But it does fully match with the "moonshot" scenario and vision of an automated world that Musk is promising to shareholders.

How TAMs could support a long term $10 Trillion valuation

To put these "moonshot" numbers in context, it is helpful to gauge what the TAMs (Total Addressable Markets) for each of Tesla's future segments could be.

Regarding Robotics, Morgan Stanley puts the humanoid robot market at $5 trillion by 2050, while Goldman Sachs estimates $38 billion by 2035. Roots Analysis projects $104 billion by 2035 at a CAGR of 47%. The extreme divergence across these estimates reflects how early-stage and hard to model this market remains. Critically, as Morgan Stanley notes, adoption likely doesn't truly accelerate until the late 2030s, which aligns with Tesla's Optimus timeline.

A long-term $234 Billion revenue for Tesla "Robotics" would mean a market share of 4.7% using Morgan Stanley's figure, below Tesla's market share in global EVs today (at around ~10%).

Concerning autonomous driving, analysts at Investing.com cite a $10 trillion robotaxi TAM as the figure underpinning Musk's bull case for Tesla's long-term valuation, making revenue figures in the $200 Billion range feasible.

As for energy, Bloomberg projects global energy storage installations growing 15-fold by 2030. Grand View Research pegs the energy storage systems market at 512 GW by 2030 (with a CAGR 11.6%), while the long-duration storage segment alone is forecast to hit $10.4 Billion by 2030. Tesla's Megapack is already a dominant player and is the most current of all these opportunities.

Further upside is achievable when accounting for Terafab

At a $10 Trillion valuation Tesla would "only" represent a ~6.6X from its current valuation (the company capitalizes ~$1.5 Trillion at the time of writing). While in itself this would be an asymmetric type of return, the Terafab is another potential major growth accelerator for Tesla, which I did not include in my $10 trillion valuation model and could therefore represent potential further upside for the company.

In line with Musk's vision of an integrated supply chain, Terafab targets a vertically integrated chip facility combining logic, memory, and advanced packaging under one roof. The stated rationale for the project is that Tesla's projected demand across AI, robotics, and space compute will exceed what TSMC, Samsung, and Micron can supply within 3 or 4 years.

Tesla's Terafab facility (joint with SpaceX) has a reported objective to produce 100-200 billion custom AI chips annually. This could eliminate dependence on NVIDIA or TSMC and potentially open a new line of business, should Tesla decide (and have capacity to) sell its chips externally.

The global AI chip market, currently dominated by NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), is worth approximately ~$500 billion depending on exact estimates. This would in itself represent another significant TAM for Tesla.

To be clear, I think it is too early to assess the impact of Terafab on Tesla's top and bottom line. Much will depend on what exactly Tesla and SpaceX can realistically achieve in terms of computing output from the project.

What I will try to better assess at earnings are the timings that surround Terafab and chips production. And these timings (at least as announced) are surprisingly close. The table below summarizes what we know so far, together with my assessment on whether or not those target dates are achievable.

What I am looking for in upcoming earnings: Optimus, FSD, Terafab

To gauge whether Musk's vision of a fully automated world is advancing (and to what extent), I will look out for three elements in the upcoming earnings:

  1. Robotics timeline confirmations or delays.

  2. Growth of FSD and robotaxis and expansion plans.

  3. Terafab-related news.

Readers will note that within the context of Q1 earnings, I am not particularly focused on Tesla's automotive revenues. That's because, in my view, the automotive-focused days of Tesla are already behind the company. I personally see Tesla's car fleet as a platform to launch robotaxis and upsell / cross-sell full-self-driving capabilities.

When it comes to robotics, I see this as the single most important catalyst for Tesla, potentially opening a new market worth at least the same in size as the company's automotive segment. Realistically, I do not expect any major announcement for Q1 2026. Production is only expected to start in late 2026 and it likely won't ramp up until well into 2027. Yet, any signal of whether the plan to produce Optimus is on track will be important.

Robotaxis are more of a short-term catalyst. To date, Tesla did not yet release any specific revenue or profitability metrics related to robotaxis. The company still reports revenue as split in three categories: automotive, energy and services (see below).

With the company allegedly focusing on robotaxi roll-out, I would expect this to change. While I wouldn't yet expect "robotaxis" to be called out as a single line item in Tesla's report for Q1, I would like to see signs of acceleration in this line of business.

Q1 earnings risks: weak car deliveries and markets pricing in a miss

Investing in Tesla means being willing to believe in Musk's long term vision. With that in mind, one has to accept the significant risks of short- and mid- term headwinds when quarterly earnings do not go as hoped.

At the time of writing, prediction markets like Polymarket are pricing in only a ~33% chance that Tesla actually beats quarterly earnings (see below). This is, in my view, likely tied to the fact that Tesla had its weakest quarter deliveries in one year in Q1 2026, with inventory swelling.

Tesla's stock has sometimes gone up even after an earnings miss. However, a bad quarter due to weak automotive results is probably the single most important risk readers have to bear in mind if they are thinking of entering a trade in Tesla ahead of Q1 earnings.

Long-term risks: if you think Tesla should trade like a car maker, buy a car maker

Other longer term risks concern the reliability and timings of all of Tesla's "moonshots", including the launch of Optimus robot and Terafab chips production. Regarding the latter, it is worth nothing that semiconductor fabs typically take 3-5 years from groundbreaking to volume production. As of April 2026, no confirmed groundbreaking date, construction partners, or equipment orders have been formally announced.

My TAM figures must also be taken with a pinch of salt. First, because these are all nascent industries and any long term forecast is inherently imprecise. And secondly because competition and Tesla's own positioning within these industries is bound to shape further in the future.

Ultimately, readers that do not believe in Musk's vision and think that Tesla should trade at a car industry standard of around 10X to 20X P/E ratio should, in my view, simply avoid Tesla and buy any traditional car manufacturer instead. An investment in Tesla only makes sense if one is to believe the long-term thesis and is willing to look beyond quarterly data.

Conclusion

Tesla is the only company in the West that can compete with Chinese conglomerates such as Xiaomi in leading both software and manufacturing of hardware at scale. It is a long term play on the West maintaining its long term edge in the "physical AI" era.

The sheer geopolitical relevance of Tesla in this context should be enough, in my opinion, to justify its rich valuation. Of course, risks abound with such an investment thesis.

Ahead of Q1 earnings, I will look out for any news and updates concerning the Terafab project, FSD / robotaxis and the production (and commercial launch) of Optimus. These are, in my view, the key catalysts that might ignite a new bull run towards my long term price target of a $10 trillion valuation. I renew my STRONG BUY rating with this long term vision in mind.

data.title: HYDB: Higher Expense Ratio For More Volatility Unappealing Amid Uncertain Market Recovery
data.description:
The iShares High Yield Systematic Bond ETF (HYDB) isn't too impressive. Despite the higher expense ratios at 0.35% and a more active strategy compared to a more passive high-yield iShares alternative like (USHY) at 0.08% (due to a value tilt in the underlying index, which tries to differentiate it from market value based indexing used in alternatives), it has not produced outperformance — a fact made stranger given the energy skew in the HYDB compared to comparable ETFs in the same asset class, where energy companies in the US would have been having a field day with the high oil prices caused by the War.

We also note the HYBD has more variance than the USHY. With markets now speculating on the war possibly ending, though the Strait remains blocked despite optimism on Friday, the HYDB could be more adapted to playing the upside due to the higher historical variance, though this is just a tactical consideration. The expense ratio is significantly higher on HYDB, with more variance and not a great relative track record. The limited value of a historically higher variance that has limited use in predicting inflection in a market recovery may not justify taking on a certainly higher expense ratio in order to play the recovery. Then there is the plain fact that high yield in general is risky right now given the uncertainty of the outcome or even the occurrence of peace talks around the Iran War.

HYDB Breakdown

Let's first have a quick look at the performance of the ETF relative to a more straightforward high-yield exposure on a YTD basis.

Despite considerably more energy skew (15% instead of 11% allocation) and fewer consumer cyclicals (16% instead of 19%), which you'd expect to be positive in a high oil price environment at the expense of consumer sentiment, the HYDB underperformed. Both the USHY and the HYDB have almost the exact same duration of 2.9 years, so that's not the explanation of the slight underperformance of the HYDB.

Using our correlation app on Python, we note less correlation of HYDB to the overall bond market as measured by the iShares Core Universal USD Bond Market ETF (IUSB). That's not great because it means the underperformance was somewhat idiosyncratic. Moreover, HYDB is also more volatile (mean-normalized standard deviation circled in red). That could explain the underperformance but also raises the question of why a more active underlying index would produce a more volatile performance since 2020, which is what our app was instructed to measure.

Theoretically, a more volatile ETF may see more upside now that the bond markets might be recovering, presuming that this round of talks under a ceasefire, if the peace talks occur, actually leads to a more enduring end to the Iran War. But that's just an assumption.

Macro Considerations

We aren't convinced that one should pick HYDB over other high yield ETFs, but disregarding these specific considerations for the moment, we also have questions about whether this recovery in bond markets will endure.

The biggest variations in YTMs transmitting through the duration effect on prices happen in high-yield ETFs from changes in credit spreads. It's not good for capital appreciation prospects from here on out that credit spreads have already normalized almost to pre-war levels, and are close to all-time lows in the last year.

Then, if the ceasefire is broken or if the talks don't succeed or occur at all and hostilities continue, that credit spread could rise again, as much as 1%, which will land together with higher benchmark rate assumptions from oil-based reinflation concerns upwards of a 3% hit to the price of the ETF based on the duration, which is not insignificant by fixed income standards.

Taking a risk-first approach, we'd highlight that communications from Iran and the US already show differing positions. Iran said it would only allow ships from nations that aren't hostile to pass, and would re-block the strait if the blockade doesn't end. The strait, as of now, is blocked meaning energy crunch risks persist. They may also endeavor to start taking their sporadic tolls again, if they haven't started already, which puts a very high floor on voyage costs in addition to all the other premia that could together keep oil prices very high. This is bad for the US economy, credit ratings, and also benchmark rates, with the specter of reinflation and persistently higher rates showing itself again. The US has made it clear that the blockade won't stop. So can the ceasefire hold under these initial conditions? It's unclear.

On the other hand, Iran has indicated that it is possible that they will come back to the negotiating table following some days of blockade, though they have been noncommittal as to their attending particularly with the US Navy having opened fire upon an Iranian commercial vessel and boarded it, actually enforcing the blockade. So tensions are escalating again. At least the ceasefire seems to be in place for the moment.

Bottom Line

Another issue is that even if the war ends, which may have limited incremental benefits from HYBD where credit spreads have already fallen so much, there is genuine concern again around the USD. The debasement trade could be coming back, especially as the war, which raised oil costs for everyone, has galvanised domestic politics in US-allied nations towards a stance that is less sympathetic to the current US policy regime, which increases fractures in the typical transatlantic alliances. The USD has already started to perform poorly again, and perhaps the debasement trade will have even more fuel now. On the other hand, if it doesn't end, credit spreads will go back up which will be the principal cause of a price hit to the HYDB.

Finally, we just aren't particularly convinced by the HYDB's relative performance despite what should have been better sector allocations than the USHY. The main reason to pick it is it's more volatile in a market recovery scenario, which isn't ordinarily a good thing, particularly when you are asked to pay a higher expense ratio cost for more volatility and when a recovery scenario remains highly uncertain. But the expense ratio is considerably higher with enough questions about value add that we'd simply look at something more passive and simple.

data.title: Lazard Developing Markets Equity Portfolio Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Equity markets in the developing world, as measured by the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, slipped 0.2% in the first quarter. The monthslong rally across equity markets in the developing world—fuelled in part by global investors seeking alternatives to US risk assets—came to an abrupt end in March due to escalating tensions in the Middle East, where the US's and Israel's joint military campaign against Iran curbed risk appetites. News that Iran had imposed a near-total blockade of the Strait of Hormuz in response to the airstrikes rattled investors, who feared that its effective closure would have dire consequences for the global economy given that one-fifth of the world's oil and natural gas and roughly one-third of the world's fertilizer that sustains global crops pass through the narrow waterway. Global stock and oil markets were volatile throughout March, as investors searched for any signs that a de-escalation of hostilities was on the horizon. The price of Brent crude oil, the global benchmark, ended March at US$118.45 per barrel, a 63% increase since the war in Iran began on 2 February, and the largest monthly jump in percentage terms on record.

Emerging Asia, which is heavily reliant on crude oil from the Middle East, was the worst-performing region in the period, falling 1.5%, due largely to worries that the oil shock will adversely impact the region's economic outlook. Nevertheless, stock markets in global technology hubs Korea and Taiwan both rose sharply, thanks to the demand upcycle for memory chips. China's stock market fell as a result of a sell-off of AI-linked stocks. India's stock market fell on concerns about the impact that surging oil prices will have on the country's economy. Indonesia's stock market tumbled on news that index provider MSCI was considering removing the country as a constituent of the MSCI Emerging Markets Index due to low liquidity and ownership concentrations, which, if carried out, would require exchange-traded funds (ETFs) to remove Indonesian company shares to match the index's composition and weighting.

Latin America was the best-performing region, rising 14.6%, thanks largely to the region's limited exposure to crude oil from the Middle East. Colombia's stock market climbed, thanks to its status as a net oil exporter. Brazil's stock market rose sharply on bullish sentiment about the country's economic outlook and expectations that the country's central bank would soon start a rate-cutting cycle. Peru's stock market gained in sympathy with rising commodity prices.

The region encompassing emerging Europe, the Middle East, and Africa (EMEA) also outperformed, despite moving sideways. South Africa's stock market fell in sympathy with the steep drop in gold prices in March and on concerns about the country's economic outlook stemming from the country's heavy dependence on crude oil from the Middle East. Saudi Arabia's stock market rose in sympathy with soaring crude oil prices.

Communication services, consumer discretionary, and real estate were the -worst-performing sectors. Information technology, energy, and utilities were the best-performing sectors.

(Index returns were measured net of taxes and in US dollar terms.)

Portfolio Review

In the first quarter, the Lazard Developing Markets Equity Portfolio rose in absolute terms and outperformed its benchmark, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index. (Excess return was measured net of fees and in US dollar terms).

Helped

  • Stock selection in energy and industrials sectors boosted relative performance, as did stock selection in Korea and Taiwan.
  • Higher-than-benchmark exposure to the information technology sector and to Brazil and Peru contributed.
  • Lower-than-benchmark exposure to the communication services and real estate sectors and to India and China was beneficial to relative performance.
  • Shares of Korea-based semiconductor manufacturer Samsung Electronics (7.5% Portfolio weighting) rose in sympathy with the rising price of memory chips stemming from strong demand.
  • Shares of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) (15.1% Portfolio weighting) traded higher on strong demand from the Taiwan-based contract chipmaker's high-performance computing (HPC) customers for the company's advanced process node manufacturing.
  • Shares of PRIO (PTRRY) (2.4% Portfolio weighting) advanced on news of the Brazil-based oil producers successful start of production at its Wal field. Shares received an additional boost from the rising price of crude oil.
  • Shares of ASE Technology (ASX) (2.1% Portfolio weighting) traded higher after the Taiwan-based chip testing and packaging provider reported better-than-expected full-year 2025 results, highlighted by earnings that grew 25% from a year earlier. Shares were boosted further after the company issued an upbeat outlook as it gains market share in advanced packaging services.
  • The rise in the stock price of Taiwan-based precision electronic interconnect components manufacturer Lotes (1.9% Portfolio weighting) was attributed to rising demand for general purpose servers.

Hurt

  • Stock selection in the materials and information technology sectors weighed on relative performance, as did stock selection in China and India.
  • Higher-than-benchmark exposure to the consumer discretionary sector and Indonesia detracted.
  • Lower-than-benchmark exposures to the materials and energy sectors and to Korea and Saudi Arabia were detrimental to relative performance.
  • The stock price of Tencent (TCEHY) (4.6% Portfolio weighting) depreciated on concerns about the China-based social media company's slower build-out of infrastructure if AI and product rollouts relative to its Chinese peers.
  • Shares of Alibaba (BABA) (3.3% Portfolio weighting) faltered on concerns about the China-based e-commerce giant's large investments in its quick commerce business segment.
  • Shares of HDFC Bank (HDB) (1.3% Portfolio weighting) faltered on news that the India-based lender's Chairman had unexpectedly stepped down from the board.
  • Shares of Alibaba (3.3% Portfolio weighting) faltered on concerns about the China-based e-commerce giant's large investments in its quick commerce business segment.
  • PharmaResearch (0.5% Portfolio weighting), a Korea-based biopharmaceutical company specializing in regenerative medicine, saw its stock price fall due to concerns about stiffening competition in the last three months of 2025.
  • Shares of non-banking finance company Bajaj Finance (0.8% Portfolio weighting), fell as part of a broader sell-off across India's stock market due to concerns about the impact the Iran war would have on the country's economic outlook. India is heavily reliant on oil import from the Middle East.

Outlook

Market fundamentals remain fluid based upon significant new and existing geopolitical and trade risks. Our expectations will vary based upon these issues and valuations, but, at this juncture, we remain reasonably constructive on long-term prospects for emerging markets, especially if global growth remains positive and balanced.

Important Information

Please consider a fund's investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses carefully before investing. For more complete information about The Lazard Funds, Inc. and current performance, you may obtain a prospectus or summary prospectus by calling 800-823-6300 or going to Lazard Asset Management . Read the prospectus or summary prospectus carefully before you invest. The prospectus and summary prospectus contain investment objectives, risks, charges, expenses, and other information about the Portfolio and The Lazard Funds that may not be detailed in this document. The Lazard Funds are distributed by Lazard Asset Management Securities LLC.

Information and opinions presented have been obtained or derived from sources believed by Lazard Asset Management LLC or its affiliates ("Lazard") to be reliable. Lazard makes no representation as to their accuracy or completeness. All opinions expressed herein are as of the published date and are subject to change.

The performance quoted represents past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. The current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. An investor may obtain performance data current to the most recent month-end online at Lazard Asset Management . The investment return and principal value of the Portfolio will fluctuate; an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost.

Different share classes may have different returns and different investment minimums.

Please click here for standardized returns:

Lazard Developing Markets Equity Portfolio

Forecasted or estimated results do not represent a promise or guarantee of future results and are subject to change.

Allocations and security selection are subject to change.

Mention of these securities should not be considered a recommendation or solicitation to purchase or sell the securities. It should not be assumed that any investment in these securities was, or will prove to be, profitable, or that the investment decisions we make in the future will be profitable or equal to the investment performance of securities referenced herein. There is no assurance that any securities referenced herein are currently held in the portfolio or that securities sold have not been repurchased. The securities mentioned may not represent the entire portfolio.

Equity securities will fluctuate in price; the value of your investment will thus fluctuate, and this may result in a loss. Securities in certain non-domestic countries may be less liquid, more volatile, and less subject to governmental supervision than in one's home market. The values of these securities may be affected by changes in currency rates, application of a country's specific tax laws, changes in government administration, and economic and monetary policy. Emerging markets securities carry special risks, such as less developed or less efficient trading markets, a lack of company information, and differing auditing and legal standards. The securities markets of emerging markets countries can be extremely volatile; performance can also be influenced by political, social, and economic factors affecting companies in these countries.

The MSCI Emerging Markets Index is a free-float-adjusted market capitalization index that is designed to measure equity market performance in the global emerging markets. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index consists of emerging markets country indices including: Brazil, Chile, China, Colombia, Czech Republic, Egypt, Greece, Hungary, India, Indonesia, Korea, Malaysia, Mexico, Peru, Philippines, Poland, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, South Africa, Taiwan, Thailand, Turkey, and United Arab Emirates. The index is unmanaged and has no fees. One cannot invest directly in an index.

Certain information included herein is derived by Lazard in part from an MSCI index or indices (the "Index Data"). However, MSCI has not reviewed this product or report,

and does not endorse or express any opinion regarding this product or report or any analysis or other information contained herein or the author or source of any such information or analysis. Due to various risks and uncertainties, actual events may differ materially from those reflected or contemplated in such forward-looking statements.
